def g():

run(g)

issue_title: _read_wait and friends are documented as being exported from top-level curio package, but they aren't
issue_body:
[This page](https://curio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference.html#low-level-kernel-system-calls) says:
```
    await curio._read_wait(fileobj, timeout=None)
```
But:
```
In [1]: import curio

In [2]: curio._read_wait
AttributeError: module 'curio' has no attribute '_read_wait'
```

It seems to be only in `curio.kernel._read_wait`. I suppose this is a better place for it and the bug is just in the docs, but either way fyi :-)

(Found because I was trying to track down what happens if a timeout expires; it doesn't seem to be mentioned in the reference manual.)

issue_title: exp and log should return dimensionless quantity
issue_body:
`exp` and `log` rightly fail with `DimensionalityError` on any quantity that has a dimension.

But when it`s passed a dimensionless quantity, it does not return a dimensionless quantity; it returns a standard numpy array (or scalar):

```
In [85]: type(numpy.exp(ureg.Quantity(1, ureg.dimensionless)))
Out[85]: numpy.float64
```

This is inconsistent with the behaviour for other ufuncs, that always return pint quantities when passed a pint quantity.  For consistency, `log`, `exp`, and perhaps some others that I've missed, should (upon success) always return a dimensionless quantity as well.

issue_title: pip install ubelt install jaraco on linux
issue_body:
The `jaraco` module is only needed on windows for the symlink functionality. As such it should not be a requirement when installing `ubelt` on Linux. However, when you `pip install ubelt` (current pypi version is at 0.2.0), it will install this package. This is undesirable because it makes it appear that ubelt is more heavyweight than it really is. 

I'm not sure why this is happening the requirement is specfied in `requirements-win32.txt` and `setup.py` should only see if `sys.platform.startswith('win32')`. Perhaps pypi is parsing the that file? Not exactly sure yet.

issue_title: Sometimes individual projects just stop working as expected
Issues with extensible matching in AD
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm trying to use extensible matching to get a list of users who are members of an AD group - in this case, I'm trying to find anyone who may be a member of the Domain Admins group either directly or via membership of another group.

My search looks like this:-

        conn.search(
            search_base=BASE_DN,
            search_filter='(&(objectClass=user)(memberOf:1.2.840.113556.1.4.1941:=CN=Domain Admins,CN=Users,DC=example,DC=com'),
            search_scope=SUBTREE,
            attributes = ALL_ATTRIBUTES
        )

When run, I get this stack trace:-

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
<...snip outer code...>
  File "/path/to/homedir/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/ldap3/core/connection.py", line 762, in search
    self.server.schema if self.server else None)
  File "/path/to/homedir/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/ldap3/operation/search.py", line 370, in search_operation
    request['filter'] = compile_filter(parse_filter(search_filter, schema, auto_escape, auto_encode).elements[0])  # parse the searchFilter string and compile it starting from the root node
  File "/path/to/homedir/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/ldap3/operation/search.py", line 230, in compile_filter
    boolean_filter[pos] = compile_filter(element)
  File "/path/to/homedir/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/ldap3/operation/search.py", line 267, in compile_filter
    matching_filter['dnAttributes'] = DnAttributes(filter_node.assertion['dnAttributes'])
  File "/path/to/homedir/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pyasn1/type/univ.py", line 103, in __init__
    base.AbstractSimpleAsn1Item.__init__(self, value, **kwargs)
  File "/path/to/homedir/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pyasn1/type/base.py", line 240, in __init__
    value = self.prettyIn(value)
  File "/path/to/homedir/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pyasn1/type/univ.py", line 254, in prettyIn
    return int(value)
TypeError: int() argument must be a string, a bytes-like object or a number, not 'NoneType'
```

Digging into the source, it looks like `DnAttributes` can only be `True` or `False`, yet at line 111 in `operation/search.py` it's initialised with `None`, but is never set to `False` if `:dn` isn't found in the filter string.

To try and get around this, I tried adding `:dn` to the search string (so `memberOf:dn:1.2.840...`), which now doesn't throw an exception but still doesn't work.

For what it's worth, running the same query using `ldapsearch` from the commandline returns the results I'd expect (both with and without the `:dn` addition).

If there's any other information I can supply, please let me know!

Andy.

issue_title: Wrong parsing of MultiGeometry when containing nested MultiGeometry
issue_body:
When parsing e.g. https://developers.google.com/kml/documentation/us_states.kml, there is a bug because you do not allow a MultiGeometry (which you consider a Geometry) to contain another MultiGeometry. The nested MultiGeometry in the US state placeholders of above .kml file are thus ignored.
Easy to fix I suspect by adding `element.findall('%sMultiGeometry' % self.ns)` line in the `_get_multigeometry` method.

issue_title: Deprecate support for Python 2.6, add support for 3.5 & 3.6
Invalid XML in <trkpt>, empty <link >
issue_body:
Using the function .to_xml() generates XML with empty <link> tags inside the <trkpt> elements (and possibly others). The gpx schema defines the link as XML LinkType, the definition requires the 'refs' attribute:

<xs:complexType mixed="true" name="LinkType">
<xs:complexContent>
<xs:extension base="baseElementType">
<xs:attribute name="refs" type="xs:IDREFS" use="required"/>
<xs:attribute name="role" type="xs:string"/>
<xs:attribute name="title" type="xs:string"/>
</xs:extension>
</xs:complexContent>
</xs:complexType>

The output trackpoints look like this:
<trkpt lat="41.3342050742358" lon="-72.67007832415402">
<ele>43.88</ele>
<time>2017-09-04T14:59:23Z</time>
<link ></link></trkpt>

The input trackpoints do not have any link tags from a valid GPX modified in Garmin Basecamp. The file does have extensions, but I don't think that matters, haven't investigated fully yet.

Python 3.6.0



[GPX In and Out.zip](https://github.com/tkrajina/gpxpy/files/1431050/GPX.In.and.Out.zip)

issue_title: Remove dependencies on scikit-bio
issue_body:
I propose to remove the dependencies on scikit-bio.

scikit-bio has recently undergo incompatible changes to the API, especially with regards to the `pcoa()` function. In addition, related to issue 57, it is still incompatible with Windows machines.

After going through the codes, I see that only the `pcoa()` and `DistanceMatrix()` functions are used from the scikit-bio package. These can be reimplemented with functions from scikit-learn only. Given the maturity of the sklearn package, it should be a good idea.

I can try to implement these portions if necessary.

issue_title: ufunc.subtract making UKF slow
issue_body: We set the residual_fn to subtract by default. However, there is code in unscented_transform that checks if this is None; if so it uses a faster computation. However, it is never set to None so the fast code does not execute. This may occur in other places in the code, I have not checked.

issue_title: HeartRate notify
issue_body:
Hi there, first of all thanks for reading this!

Currently i am working on a heart rate monitor in python with the polar H7 and i'm getting stuck with the notify part of BLE heart rate.

```
import struct
import btle


class MyDelegate(btle.DefaultDelegate):
    def __init__(self):
        btle.DefaultDelegate.__init__(self)

    def handleNotification(self, cHandle, data):
        print(data)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    heartrate_uuid = btle.UUID(0x2a38)

p = btle.Peripheral("00:22:D0:2B:F8:EC")
p.setDelegate(MyDelegate())

print("Connected")

try:
    print("Setting Characteristics")
    ch = p.getCharacteristics(uuid=heartrate_uuid)[0]
    print("Setting Done, writing now")
    ch.write(struct.pack('<bb', 0x01, 0x00))

issue_title: keepalive.HTTPResponse.read decodes response body as UTF-8
issue_body: I would expect that the `keepalive.HTTPResponse.read` (overriding `httplib.HTTPResponse.read`) method returns str objects. Instead, it returns unicode objects. In fact, it decodes the body as `UTF-8` in all cases. It shouldn't do that, because the encoding of the body may be different or the body may not even be text.

issue_title: spooler error on py3.6 with priority defined
issue_body:
Calling a spooled method declared with a decorator:

`@task(executor=TaskExecutor.SPOOLER, priority=2)`

Throws error:

```
File "/apps/pocket-e/003/env/live/lib/python3.6/site-packages/uwsgi_tasks/tasks.py" in __call__
  624.         task.execute_async()

File "/apps/pocket-e/003/env/live/lib/python3.6/site-packages/uwsgi_tasks/tasks.py" in execute_async
  357.         return uwsgi.spool(self.get_message_content())

Exception Type: ValueError
Exception Value: spooler callable dictionary must contains only bytes
```

Inspecting the payload passed to uwsgi.spool() method:

```
{b'function_name': b'sentry.tasks.spooled_process_setup_data', b'args': b'\x80\x03K\x01\x85q\x00.', b'kwargs': b'\x80\x03}q\x00.', b'setup': b'\x80\x03}q\x00(X\x08\x00\x00\x00priorityq\x01K\x02X\x0b\x00\x00\x00working_dirq\x02X\x1e\x00\x00\x00/apps/pocket-e/003/pocketServeq\x03X\x0b\x00\x00\x00retry_countq\x04K\x00u.', b'priority': 2}
```

Note value of 'priority' is straight int here and not encoded bytes.

Adding the following at line 338 in tasks.py fixes the issue (but I'm not super happy with that hack fix so feel free to do it smarter please...):

```
                if isinstance(s, int):
                    return bytes([s])
```

issue_title: Allow request body on DELETE
issue_body:
According to https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7231#section-4.3.5

> A payload within a DELETE request message has no defined semantics;
   sending a payload body on a DELETE request might cause some existing
   implementations to reject the request.

There is no reason to avoid a body in DELETE requests

```python
@method_decorator(name='destroy', decorator=swagger_auto_schema(
    request_body='list of ids',
    responses={status.HTTP_204_NO_CONTENT: 'No content'}
))
>>> drf_yasg.errors.SwaggerGenerationError: request_body can only be applied to (PUT,PATCH,POST)
```

I tried to submit a pull request updating 
```python
class ViewInspector(BaseInspector):
    body_methods = ('PUT', 'PATCH', 'POST', 'DELETE')  #: methods that are allowed to have a request body
```
but many tests fail and I would need help with that.

Thanks for this really good lib :)

issue_title: Currency pair classes
issue_body:
I have an use-case where I'm comparing different exchange rates. From [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Currency_pair): A currency pair is EUR/USD. Here, EUR is the base currency and USD is the quote currency. The quotation EUR/USD 1.2500 means that one euro is exchanged for 1.2500 US dollars. 

I'd like to write something like

```
# Convert here 1 EUR for 1 USD
exchange1 = CurrencyPair(base = 'EUR', quote = 'USD', rate = '1.25')

# Convert here 1 EUR for 2 USD
exchange2 = CurrencyPair(base = 'EUR', quote = 'USD', rate = '2.50')

euro = Money(amount='1.00', currency='EUR')
dollar1 = exchange1 * euro   # dollar1 is now equal to Money(amount='1.25', currency='USD')
dollar2 = exchange2 * euro   # dollar2 is now equal to Money(amount='2.50', currency='USD')
```

I made this quickly by duplicating the `Money` class and changing some of it's multiplication operations.

Is this something you'd like to support? If so, I can make a proper pull request.

issue_title: Traversing typedef struct issue
Swap Adar I and Adar II in Hebrew
issue_body:
Hello, a nice work you've done here!

I wanted to use this tool for our python-powered site and noticed that Adar months in leap year are not where they're supposed to be. The thing is that in Hebrew calendar Adar I is additional. I.e., a person born in Adar in a common Hebrew year would celebrate his birthday in Adar Bet (the second) in a leap year. Same thing with the Holidays.

So this way, `hebrew.from_gregorian()` should return consequently ... — 10 — 11 — 13 — 12— 1 — ... for a leap year. It might seem irrational, but *this* is the way Hebrew calendar works.

Since your package is on PyPI, I realize that this change will result in existing apps using this library not working properly when they update the library. But I assume nobody yet used it for Hebrew, because they would definitely notice the issue.

Although, I could make a pull request, but later, when I'm less busy.

Let's say a row contains "Romeo or Julia":

Filter: "Romeo or " is not working

Let's say a row contains "Romeo 123 Julia":

Filter: "Romeo 123 Julia" is working

issue_title: dropped messages
issue_body:
What about dropped messages?  GDAX mentions:

> While a websocket connection is over TCP, the websocket servers receive market data in a manner which can result in dropped messages. Your feed consumer should either be designed to expect and handle sequence gaps and out-of-order messages, or use channels that guarantee delivery of messages.

Certain other exchanges make similar warnings about out of order/dropped messages, although I cannot currently recall which ones exactly.

The `message_handler` currently only checks if `msg_seq < last_seen_seq`. Unless I am missing something, it would seem this can result in an inaccurate order book if `msg_seq > last_seen_seq + 1`. The simple solution here is to call `_book_snapshot` upon detecting this. 

So in message_handler:

                last_seen_seq = self.seq_no[msg['product_id']]
                msg_seq = msg['sequence']
                if msg_seq < last_seen_seq:
                    # out of order message, ignore
                    return
                elif msg_seq > (last_seen_seq + 1):
                    LOG.warning(
                        'Missing messages for {} (Last seq: {}, received seq: {}).'.format(
                            msg['product_id'],
                            last_seen_seq,
                            msg_seq
                        )
                    )
                    await self._book_snapshot()  # rebuild book after missing msg detected
                    return

issue_title: Use a common tests3/sqlservertests.py for multiple drivers?
issue_body:
I noticed that there was a tests2/freetdstests.py but no tests3/freetdstests.py. I wanted to see how up-to-date the FreeTDS support was, so instead of cloning tests2/freetdstests.py (which seemed quite old) I cloned tests3/sqlservertests.py and applied the minimal tweaks it to make it run. It worked quite well: out of 208 tests there were only 3 errors and 2 failures.

Given the massive duplication between my tests3/freetdstests.py and tests3/sqlservertests.py, it occurs to me that it might be good to simply add conditional logic to tests3/sqlservertests.py for multiple SQL Server drivers. We could add a function to inspect `cnxn.getinfo(pyodbc.SQL_DRIVER_NAME)` to see if we're using

- msodbcsql ("ODBC Driver x for SQL Server"),
- FreeTDS, or
- some other driver (e.g., OpenLink's offering)

... and then tweak the tests accordingly.

Does that sound like the best approach?

issue_title: Cannot connect using a password ending with a semicolon
issue_body:
The following connection string, where the password ends with a semicolon e.g.:

`cs = "DRIVER={SQL Server};server=SQLEXPRESS;database=dbname;uid=user;pwd=pwd;"`

In .NET the web.config handles this by allowing the password to be wrapped in quotes e.g.

`cs = "DRIVER={SQL Server};server=SQLEXPRESS;database=dbname;uid=user;pwd='pwd;';"`

This doesn't seem supported by pyodbc however and returns:

`[Microsoft][ODBC SQL Server Driver][SQL Server]Login failed for user 'user'. (18456) (SQLDriverConnect); [01S00] [Microsoft][ODBC Driver Manager] Invalid connection string attribute (0); [28000] [Microsoft][ODBC SQL Server Driver][SQL Server]Login failed for user`

issue_title: include directives mismatch / nonexistent
issue_body:
I don't know where this would be getting set / changed, but with doxygen if you have a nested folder with a file in it (e.g. `include/arbitrary/common.h`), when you have say a `struct` in that file you need to add the following documentation:

```
/**
 * \struct some_struct common.h arbitrary/common.h
 * \brief A struct that represents something.
 */
struct some_struct { ... };
```
Without the `\struct some_struct common...` part, the generated html from doxygen will just say `#include <common.h>`, but if you add that line it will say `#include <arbitrary/common.h>` and link it just fine.

With the breathe output:
- `union` will have an `#include` statement, but not incorporate the modified path.
- `struct` and `class` will not present an `#include` statement at all.

Right now I'm just manually adding a line above `struct` and `class` saying where they come from, and avoiding the `union` directive and enumerating its members.  Is this operator error, or something that breathe is missing?

issue_title: PGN parser performance
issue_body:
I prepare some sandbox code to read large pgn database but found performance problems.

Can I improve this code and merge into python chess (if will have time for it) or you will fix it little in future?

See if for 100 pgn is 3 so for 1 million will be 30 000 seconds

http://www.top-5000.nl/pgn.htm

```
# coding=utf-8
import chess.pgn
import os

def read_games():
  pgn_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__),
                          os.path.pardir,
                          os.path.pardir,
                          'data', 'millionbase-2.22.pgn')

  counter = 0
  with open(pgn_path) as pgn_file:
    while True:
      game = chess.pgn.read_game(pgn_file)
      if game == None:
        break
      counter += 1
      if counter == 100:
        break
      if counter % 10 == 0:
        print counter

import cProfile, pstats, StringIO
pr = cProfile.Profile()
pr.enable()

read_games()

pr.disable()
s = StringIO.StringIO()
sortby = 'tottime'
ps = pstats.Stats(pr, stream=s).sort_stats(sortby)
ps.print_stats()
print s.getvalue()
```

issue_title: Allow for rules that don't create separate nodes
issue_body:
It's often useful to define some basic rules that can be composited to form larger expressions:

```
digit = ~r"[0-9]"
number = digit+
```

However, there is not always any reason why each separate `digit` needs its own node, even if it's useful as a "building block" in the grammar. As such, it would be nice to be able to mark a rule as "non-node creating". The library simpleparse supports this by creating '[unreported productions](http://simpleparse.sourceforge.net/simpleparse_grammars.html)' that would translate into something like this for parsimonious:

```
<digit> = ~r"[0-9]"
number = digit+
```

Then, any `number` node could simply be the number, and we don't get a bunch of (unneeded) nodes for each component – because the angle brackets signify that this rule should not be "reported" separately.

I suppose this could either be implemented while parsing the grammar – replacing any reference to such a rule with simply its contents (at a preprocessing stage), or as part of the parsing of the text...

issue_title: Backrefs should handle `\N{name}` in search and replace
issue_body:
Not sure how this escaped me, in passing it was mentioned that [Python may support `\N` in replace](https://github.com/facelessuser/backrefs/issues/7) in the future, and I realized, that I don't support that in search.  We should add support for `\N{name}` in search patterns.

We should also support this in replace like `regex` does.  Hopefully one day stuff like this will be supported in `re` so we can drop this kind of support.

This should be straight forward. Search will capture the pattern `\N{name of  char}` and use unicodedata to find the character.  Now we could create a table ahead of time, but I imagine that the procedure in unicodedata is much the same but faster, so I don't see any sense in wasting time in a vain attempt to speed things up when most likely it is already very fast.

Replace should work the same way as we handle `\U` or `\u`. Format replace patterns will work the same, but we will have to capture `\N{name}` before we process `{key}`, but that should happen naturally.  I don't think we should require escaping `\N{{name}}`. Considering that a normal format strings operates this way, I think this is the best approach:

```py
>>> '{} {} \N{solidus}'.format('test', 'this')
'test this /'
```

issue_title: Should the replace() function be renamed?
issue_body:
The name makes sense for `namedtuple`, where the fields are truly replaced. But now that the function creates a new object using `__init__` and not longer has the post-copy functionality, it doesn't really make sense for Data Classes. For example the fact that `init=False` fields are not copied from the original object makes `replace()` a name that doesn't really make sense.

`attrs` calls the equivalent function `evolve()`, which I don't really like, either. But I don't have a good name to offer up. Maybe I'll leave it `replace()`, and let python-dev argue about it when I post the PEP there. That should generate a few hundred emails!

For what it's worth, `attrs` has an API that's similar to `namedtuple`'s `replace()`. They call that one `assoc()`, but it's deprecated. I don't think that name makes sense, either.

issue_title: Not using O_NODELAY reduce performance considerably
issue_body:
`connection = yield from asynqp.connect(sock=sock)` give RTT 0.04 sec (!) (i.e. 22 ops/sec)

```
sock = socket.socket()
sock.connect(('localhost', 5672))
sock.setsockopt(socket.IPPROTO_TCP, socket.TCP_NODELAY, 1)
```

give RTT 0.004 (i.e. 234 ops/sec)

RPC libraries should DIFINITELY set O_NODELAY on sockets.

issue_title: Confusing ChoiceItem behavior
issue_body:
Turns out that values and labels are created differently than I'd expect:
```
class Choices(DjangoChoices):
    foo = ChoiceItem(_("Footastic"))
    bar = ChoiceItem(_("Baraboom"))

In this example, `foo` and `bar` are labels and `Footastic` and `Baraboom` are values - I'd expect the opposite behavior.

I also noticed that explicitly passingi the label is not enough
```
foo = ChoceItem(label=_("Footastic"))

because then both values and labels are "Footastic"

Why labels, instead of values, are inferred from the attribute name?

issue_title: Use `request.full_path` instead of `request.path`
issue_body:
I have some problem with `flask.request.path` in [this line](https://github.com/cameronbwhite/Flask-CAS/blob/10ee70466ac9e71cec3602c1cd46f0566618f67e/flask_cas/__init__.py#L99)

 flask.session['CAS_AFTER_LOGIN_SESSION_URL'] = flask.request.full_path

issue_title: Request: optionally allow remaining bytes in stream
issue_body:
The JavaObjectUnmarshaller.readObject method currently emits a warning when there are remaining bytes present beyond the serialized Java object. It would be useful to have a way to suppress that warning in cases where further data in the input stream is expected and normal.

Motivating use case is unserializing SecretKey objects from JCEKS keystores, as done in the pyjks project (https://github.com/doublereedkurt/pyjks). The JCEKS keystore format is almost entirely custom structured binary data, with the exception of SecretKey entries which are stored as wholesale serialized Java objects embedded right in the middle of the file.

Keystore entries can appear in any order and mix of types, and there are always raw signature bytes present at the end of a keystore, so it is fully expected for there to be trailing data after a call to readObject. There is no way of knowing in advance what the size of a serialized Java object is either, so we also can't get around it by feeding a subset of the stream to javaobj.

So my request is: could we have a flag to indicate that trailing data is expected, in which case no warning is emitted?

issue_title: Templatetag js_reverse_inline breaks on Django 1.9
issue_body:
I'm using the `{% js_reverse_inline %}` template tag to put the script on a couple of pages in a project. Those pages started failing with a JavaScript SyntaxError once the project was updated from Django 1.8 to 1.9, which looks to be because of [this change in template tag escaping](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.9/releases/1.9/#simple-tag-now-wraps-tag-output-in-conditional-escape). The output of the `simple_tag` is escaped by default now and a SyntaxError is raised when the parser hits `&&`.

You can work around the error by turning autoescape off around the tag:

```html
{% autoescape off %}
<script type="text/javascript" charset="utf-8">{% js_reverse_inline %}</script>
{% endautoescape %}
```

Looking at the release notes, the proper fix seems to be to wrap the return value of `js_reverse_inline` with `mark_safe`.

issue_title: Server subclassing Thread breaks Thread internals
issue_body:
I was trying to figure out where this really mysterious error was coming from:
```
Exception ignored in: <module 'threading' from '/usr/lib/python3.6/threading.py'>
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/threading.py", line 1294, in _shutdown
    t.join()
TypeError: join() missing 1 required positional argument: 'url'
```

It turns out that at shutdown time, the interpreter automatically calls the `join` method on every non-daemon thread. However, pytest-httpbin's `Server` class is a `Thread` subclass that overrides the `join` method to mean something else entirely, so everyone gets very confused.

Suggested fix: instead of subclassing `Thread`, `Server` should have-a `Thread` instance.

Exists method returning a false positive
issue_body:
The exists method returns a false positive when a file with a similar name exists within the bucket.

I did comment on the comment on the commit in question but have copied is here for completeness.

https://github.com/etianen/django-s3-storage/commit/027dcdf4185f97d8317965e6450d5378ddc5155b

```
This causes false positives for keys that have the same prefix.

"css/main.css.map" existing on s3 for "css/main.css" would cause this to return true.

Do we need to check for directory existence in this method? the docs state this is just for files?
```
Looking further this looks like it was changed in relation to #16 the [Django docs](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.9/ref/files/storage/#django.core.files.storage.Storage.exists
) does state 

> Returns True if a file referenced by the given name already exists in the storage system, or False if the name is available for a new file.

But is is used with a directory in the `collectstatic --clear` command: https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/contrib/staticfiles/management/commands/collectstatic.py#L106

issue_title: How to prevent unicode strings being passed as search parameters
issue_body:
Not at all sure this is the right place to ask.. I'm running django 1.11.12 and dango-pyodbc-azure 1.11.12.1 with driver "ODBC Driver 13 for SQL Server" on Windows 10 and it work fine. However, when running queries via Django's ORM,  i noticed about a 3x slowdown using certain queries that convert to LIKE in sql (e.g. field__startswith). Pinned it down in sql profiler to query parameters all being passed in as N'parameter', even when the underlying db column is not unicode. For examle, sql profiler output may show:
```
declare @p1 int
set @p1=6
exec sp_prepexec @p1 output,N'@P1 nvarchar(6)',N'SELECT TOP 1 [dbo].[MyView].[MyVarcharColumn] FROM [dbo].[MyView] WHERE [dbo].[MyView].[MyVarcharColumn] LIKE @P1 ESCAPE ''\''',N'Hello%'
select @p1
```
Column MyVarcharColumn really is a varchar column. I've tried many things, including encoding strings to ascii and passing various driver flags via 'extra_params' (such as "Auto Translate", "driver_charset", "collation" etc) and also tried "SQL Server Native Client 11.0" and setting conn.setencoding and connsetdecoding to 'latin1' in base.py as an attempt to use what's stated [here](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/wiki/Unicode). Would really appreciate any pointers on this.

import glob, os

path =  os.getcwd()

issue_title: have_been_called_with could fail if any argument has custom __eq__ method
issue_body:
The problem is with this line: https://github.com/jaimegildesagredo/doublex-expects/blob/master/doublex_expects/matchers.py#L97

If any of your arguments have a custom `__eq__` method it could fail. For example:

```
def __eq__(self, other):
    return self.id == other.id
```

issue_title: [R] Bug introduced in initializer between 0.10.1 and 0.11.1
issue_body:
Issue with MXNet R package, first observed in 0.11.1. Compiled on Ubuntu 16.04 and 14.04. 

The initializers (uniform, normal, Xavier) now return all near 0s. 
The bugs appear to come from the `mx.runif` and `mx.rnorm` in Random.R, which themselves rely on `mx.nd.internal.sample.uniform` and `mx.nd.internal.sample.normal`. 

The usage of the `random` functions such as `mx.nd.random.uniform` instead of the `sample` functions seems more appropriate for the initialization task. Right now, as all weights are initialized to zeros, no model training is possible. 

I'll open a pull with a quick fix for the initializers.

import mxnet as mx
import numpy as np
from word2vec_io import BucketSentenceIter

    label = label.T.reshape((-1,))

issue_body:
## Description
Following http://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/how_to/finetune.html
Use mx.image.ImageIter to read lst files instead.
Failed with  " src/io/image_io.cc:168: Check failed: static_cast<void*>(dst.ptr()) == out->data().dptr_"

MAINTAINER dwSun

RUN apt-get update
RUN apt-get install python3-pip libgfortran3 -y
RUN pip3 install mxnet-cu90mkl jupyter matplotlib pandas ipython scikit-image -i https://pypi.douban.com/simple/ && rm -rvf ~/.cache

RUN jupyter notebook --generate-config
RUN sed "s/#c.NotebookApp.token = '<generated>'/c.NotebookApp.token = 'mx_cuda'/" /root/.jupyter/jupyter_notebook_config.py -i

RUN mkdir /code
WORKDIR /code

EXPOSE 8888
CMD jupyter notebook --port=8888 --ip 0.0.0.0 --no-browser --allow-root
```

```sh

```

mx_train.py:
```py
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import logging
import mxnet as mx

def get_iterators(batch_size, data_shape=(3, 224, 224)):
    train = mx.image.ImageIter(
        path_imglist='./caltech-256-60-train.lst',
        data_name='data',
        label_name='softmax_label',
        path_root='./caltech_256_train_60',
        batch_size=batch_size,
        data_shape=data_shape,
        shuffle=True,
        rand_crop=True,
        rand_mirror=True)
    val = mx.image.ImageIter(
        path_imglist='./caltech-256-60-val.lst',
        data_name='data',
        label_name='softmax_label',
        path_root='./256_ObjectCategories',
        batch_size=batch_size,
        data_shape=data_shape,
        rand_crop=False,
        rand_mirror=False)
    return (train, val)

def get_fine_tune_model(symbol, arg_params, num_classes, layer_name='flatten0'):
    all_layers = symbol.get_internals()
    net = all_layers[layer_name + '_output']
    net = mx.symbol.FullyConnected(data=net, num_hidden=num_classes, name='fc1')
    net = mx.symbol.SoftmaxOutput(data=net, name='softmax')
    new_args = dict({k: arg_params[k] for k in arg_params if 'fc1' not in k})
    return (net, new_args)

def fit(symbol, arg_params, aux_params, train, val, batch_size):
    devs = mx.gpu()
    mod = mx.mod.Module(symbol=symbol, context=devs)
    mod.fit(train, val,
            num_epoch=2,
            arg_params=arg_params,
            aux_params=aux_params,
            allow_missing=True,
            batch_end_callback=mx.callback.Speedometer(batch_size, 10),
            kvstore='device',
            optimizer='sgd',
            optimizer_params={'learning_rate': 0.01},
            initializer=mx.init.Xavier(rnd_type='gaussian', factor_type="in", magnitude=2),
            eval_metric='acc')
    metric = mx.metric.Accuracy()
    return mod.score(val, metric)

num_classes = 256
batch_size = 32
head = '%(asctime)-15s %(message)s'
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG, format=head)
sym, arg_params, aux_params = mx.model.load_checkpoint('resnet-50', 0)

(new_sym, new_args) = get_fine_tune_model(sym, arg_params, num_classes)
(train, val) = get_iterators(batch_size)
mod_score = fit(new_sym, new_args, aux_params, train, val, batch_size)
print(mod_score)

issue_title: Rapidly sending messages leads to loss of messages
issue_body:
Using zeromq 4.1, pyzqmq 16.0.2, ubuntu 16.04
 
Here is a scenario where a listener binds to a port and then multiple senders come in quick succession and send a message:
```
def test():
    port = 8876
    ctx = zmq.Context.instance()
    listener = ctx.socket(zmq.ROUTER)
    listener.setsockopt(zmq.LINGER, 1)
    listener.bind('tcp://*:{}'.format(port))
    fds = set()
    und = set()
    count = 10
    for i in range(count):
        sock = ctx.socket(zmq.DEALER)
        sock.setsockopt(zmq.LINGER, 1)
        sock.connect('tcp://127.0.0.1:{}'.format(port))
        print(sock.FD)
        fds.add(sock.FD)
        und.add(sock.underlying)
        sock.send('m:{}:{}:{}'.format(i, sock.FD, sock.underlying).encode())
    print(len(fds), len(und))
    r = 0
    while r != count:
        try:
            print(listener.recv_multipart(flags=zmq.NOBLOCK))
            r += 1
        except:
            time.sleep(.01)
            continue
    print('got all')
    print('done')
```

issue_title: creation of datetime objects in riak ts record sets should be optional
issue_body:
When returning record sets from Riak TS queries, the client automatically converts [Riak TS timestamps] (https://github.com/basho/riak-python-client/blob/ce5901fdde37efa2d3f576ac895b63457fe9a025/riak/transports/pbc/codec.py#L749) into `datetime` objects and vice versa. 

For many applications where Python is an intermediate layer, converting into a `datetime` is not required.

Additionally there are some timestamps that do not roundtrip properly.

The first of which is 1001 (code taken from https://github.com/basho/riak-python-client/blob/2.4.2/riak/transports/pbc/codec.py#L86 )

```
In [69]: d._unix_time_millis(d._datetime_from_unix_time_millis(1001))
Out[69]: 1000
```

It would be nice if the conversion to `datetime` objects as optional.

issue_title: HdfsAtomicWritePipe silently fails when target file exists
issue_body:
Lately due to some pipeline issues I've ended up re-running a lot of jobs while their output exists. They seem to run fine but the output doesn't change or even get an updated modified time. I tracked this down to the atomic write pipe just silently failing when the move does nothing. I use the snakebite client with hdfs fallback. I believe this is happening in the snakebite client.

Demonstration code:

```
import luigi.contrib.hdfs

target = luigi.contrib.hdfs.HdfsTarget('/tmp/test.txt')
with target.open('w') as fobj:
    fobj.write('test1')

try:
    with target.open('w') as fobj:
        fobj.write('test2')
finally:
    with target.open() as fobj:
        print '\ncontents:', fobj.read()
    target.remove()
```
I would expect to either see `test2` printed. At the very least, I'd expect to see an error message if it prints `test1`, as this means the second write didn't work. Instead I see `test1` and no error message. So it looks like I successfully wrote `test2` to hdfs when I didn't.

issue_title: bug in SelectMultipleType.shares_exactly_one_element_with
issue_body:
This function will always return `True`, except when `other_value` is empty and the for loop is skipped entirely.
 
```py
@type_operator(FIELD_SELECT_MULTIPLE)
def shares_exactly_one_element_with(self, other_value):
    found_one = False
    select = SelectType(self.value)
    for other_val in other_value:
        if select.contains(other_val):
            if found_one:
                return False
            found_one = True
    return found_one
```

The current logic is equivalent to:
```py
return len(other_value) > 0
```

issue_title: Handle multiple connections more gracefully
issue_body:
Enhancement Request:

Right now if I login to 2 vCloud endpoints (clouda.provider.com and cloudb.provider.com) in separate sessions, the output from vcd-cli commands (e.g. vcd vdc list) shows only vdcs from the most recently logged in session (cloudb.provider.com).

e.g.
Terminal Window #1 - login to clouda.provider.com
Terminal Window #2 - login to cloudb.provider.com
Terminal Window #1 - vcd vdc list - gets back the list of VDCs in cloudb.provider.com

Ideally vcd-cli should be able to cope gracefully with multiple connections and list all resources from all available connections (similar to how Connect-CIServer behaves in PowerCLI).

For bonus points - if the providers are linked in a vCD v9.x Multi Site configuration where the orgs are paired it would be great to be able to operate on both linked sites with a single login (e.g. vcd login to clouda.provider.com which is linked to cloudb.provider.com and have commands able to see/operate against resources in both endpoints).

Not sure how much work is involved in this, as the static/global single session ID would need to become an array of connected endpoints (so I suspect it is non-trivial), but would save a lot of confusion for those of us working with multiple connected endpoints.

issue_title: Serializer still depends on GDAL?
issue_body:
I encountered a strange issue. On my development machine, everything worked fine. When I tried to run my project on another computer, I suddenly got errors like the following when saving a PointField:
```python
>>> self.pointField = {'type': 'Point', 'coordinates': [lon, lat]}
>>> self.save()
[2016-28-23 20:28:53] ERROR [django.contrib.gis:45] Error creating geometry from value '{
  "type":"Point",
  "coordinates":[
    7.09,
    50.72
  ]
}' (Initializing geometry from JSON input requires GDAL.)

After performing a `sudo apt-get install gdal-bin` (which also installed a whole bunch of other geospatial libraries), the errors were gone. But the whole point of using django-geojson is that I don't need to install geospatial libraries just to store some Points and have nice Leaflet widgets.

On both computers, Django runs in the same freshly installed Python3-virtualenv:

issue_title: Address likely FAQs about Pipfile in the README
issue_body:
I know this project was just open-sourced and is still under active development, so consider this a reminder to update the README with some additional info when the time is right.

Some questions that immediately popped into my mind, which others are likely to ask, include:

1. How does `Pipfile` relate to `setup.py` and the [upcoming `pyproject.toml`](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0518/)? Is it the same relationship as the one [described here](https://caremad.io/posts/2013/07/setup-vs-requirement/) between `requirements.txt` and `setup.py`?
2. With the planned `setup.py` to `pyproject.toml` transition, we seem to be moving away from using Python and more towards using a declarative syntax for specifying build system requirements. With this proposed `requirements.txt` to `Pipfile` transition, however, we seem to be moving in the opposite direction. Could you comment on this?
3. Does `Pipfile` just use a Python-like syntax, or will it actually run Python code, like `setup.py`?
4. What is the purpose of `Pipfile.lock`, and will packaging end-users need to care about this file?

issue_title: Parsing Finnish version of "on monday", "in October"
issue_body:
dateparser cannot recognize certain Finnish versions of days and months.

**Code that reproduces the issue**

```
In[2]: import dateparser
In[3]: dateparser.parse('tiistaina, 27. lokakuuta 2015')
```

**Expected behavior**

The above code should return `datetime.datetime(2015, 10, 27, 0, 0)`

**Observed behavior**

The above code returns `None`.

Notice that dateparser correctly deals with regular forms:

```
In[4]: dateparser.parse('tiistai, 27. lokakuu 2015')
Out[4]: 
datetime.datetime(2015, 10, 27, 0, 0)
```

It also deals with similar cases in English:

```
In[5]: dateparser.parse('on friday')
Out[5]: 
datetime.datetime(2016, 10, 7, 0, 0)
In[6]: dateparser.parse('in October')
Out[6]: 
datetime.datetime(2016, 10, 14, 0, 0)
```

**Notes**

More details on this stackoverflow question: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/33375709/python-strptime-finnish
Link to list of time expressions in Finnish: http://people.uta.fi/~km56049/finnish/timexp.html

issue_title: Mapping value indicator (":") and flow context
issue_body:
Hello.

I think I may have found a slight deviation from the spec:

In [7.4.2. Flow Mappings](http://yaml.org/spec/1.2/spec.html#id2790832)
The use of ": " is defined as:
```
c-ns-flow-map-separate-value(n,c)	::=	“:” /* Not followed by an
       ns-plain-safe(c) */
```

ns-plain-safe(c) definition is dependent on the specific flow context.

```
[127] ns-plain-safe(c)	::=	
   c = flow-out  ⇒ ns-plain-safe-out
   c = flow-in   ⇒ ns-plain-safe-in
   c = block-key ⇒ ns-plain-safe-out
   c = flow-key  ⇒ ns-plain-safe-in	

[128]	ns-plain-safe-out	::=	ns-char	 
[129]	ns-plain-safe-in	::=	ns-char - c-flow-indicator
```

In [7.4. Flow Collection Styles](http://yaml.org/spec/1.2/spec.html#id2790088) the flow-out and flow-in are defined as: 
```
A flow collection may be nested within a block collection
(flow-out context), nested within another flow collection (flow-in context)
```

My understanding of this is the **c-flow-indicators** are only allowed to **immediately** follow a colon in a **nested** flow (flow-in) context. 

So while the following is legal YAML:
```yaml
# ":" followed by flow indicator "}"  inside another flow collection (flow-in).
- [1, {a:} ,3]
```

The following is **not** valid YAML:
```yaml
# ":" followed by flow indicator "}"  inside a block collection (flow-out).
- {a:}
```

However with JSYaml's online demo **both** of these are valid.

I'm not 100% sure I understood this correctly as YAML's is somewhat overcomplicated 😞 ...
Is this a real bug or a misunderstand on my part?

issue_title: Better package for graphviz?
issue_body:
Lately I've had fairly frequent build failures on Windows due to the Chocolatey package we're using for graphviz, which downloads from graphviz.org on every install.  However, graphviz.org appears to be somewhat unreliable, resulting in timeouts, and thus botched builds.

I'm a little surprised the file isn't just cached by AppVeyor, but who knows how aggressively they cache.

So I wanted to see if we could point Chocolatey to a better URL for graphviz, perhaps a download we would just host ourselves.  According to the Chocolatey folks that is [not possible at this time](https://chocolatey.org/packages/graphviz.portable#comment-2151525358), but we could make our own copy of the package, with our own download URL, and point `choco install` to our custom package, which would be fine I guess.

issue_title: problem with dtype float limit checking
issue_body:
## Description
After loading a certain image and doing some preprocessing I tried to do a watershed transform like the one described here: http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/auto_examples/plot_marked_watershed.html

During the processing, an error kept showing up after calling filters.rank.gradient(), with the following traceback:

`/home/thypad/local/miniconda3/envs/anchor/lib/python2.7/site-packages/skimage/util/dtype.pyc in convert(image, dtype, force_copy, uniform)
    203     if kind_in == 'f':
    204         if np.min(image) < -1.0 or np.max(image) > 1.0:
--> 205             raise ValueError("Images of type float must be between -1 and 1.")
    206         if kind == 'f':
    207             # floating point -> floating point

ValueError: Images of type float must be between -1 and 1.`

I checked a bunch of times, and the image passed is float64, but then I discovered that with this specific steps and image, the image I pass to filters.rank.gradient() has image.max() equal to 1.0000000002 or something like that, so it fails on this condition on module dtype.py.

I'm not sure it's necessary for me to inform how to reproduce the bug, because it's somewhat obvious that this condition checking for floats fails in the situation I just said, and the situation is possible, and appeared right after running filters.gaussian(image).

I just checked the dtype.py module on the repo, and this condition checking is still there. My suggestion is changing it so that it will take into account small errors in floating-point precision.

issue_title: Config Parser default encoding should be utf-8
issue_body:
With non ascii characters in `config-files.ini` (e.g. in comments) we get a `UnicodeDecodeError: 'ascii' codec can't decode byte ....`. this is because the default encoding of the configparser is ascii. My suggestion is to change the default encoding to utf-8. 

This would be done by setting `"utf-8"` in line 90 in `konfig/__init__.py `. Change from:

```python
        if isinstance(filename, basestring):
            self.filename = filename
            self.read(filename)
```

to:

```python
        if isinstance(filename, basestring):
            self.filename = filename
            self.read(filename, "utf-8")
```

It's a small change with huge effect outside of the ascci-world ;-)

issue_title: dbdump: check disk space before processing
issue_body:
Originally on 2011-04-01

Currently dbdump can rotate its dump files via the N argument.  The
rotation happens after a new dump was taken, so actually the required
temporary disk space is N+1 times the size of the dump file.

It will be good to check available disk space before processing.  So,
look for latest dump file and estimate whether it is safe to make a
new dump.  If OK, continue.  If not OK, alert the admin (by
registering an exception) but do not stop the queue with an error,
because the Invenio site can still operate normally.  (So this is a
continuable error situation.)

Note that on some sites, the DB dump space lives on an AFS folder, so
the disk free space estimation should use either (i) `fs listq`
command, or if it is a regular Unix filesystem, (ii) `df -k` command.

issue_title: tests: simplify doctest execution
issue_labels: [{'id': 414884171, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio-assets/labels/Size:%20easy', 'name': 'Size: easy', 'color': 'bc3cbc', 'default': False}, {'id': 414884119, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio-assets/labels/Status:%20ready%20for%20work', 'name': 'Status: ready for work', 'color': '149414', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
The following cookiecutter change:

   https://github.com/inveniosoftware/cookiecutter-invenio-module/pull/98

should be propagated to this Invenio module.

Namely, in `run-tests.sh`, the sphinx for doctests is invoked after pytest run:

```console
$ tail -3 ./\{\{\ cookiecutter.project_shortname\ \}\}/run-tests.sh
sphinx-build -qnNW docs docs/_build/html && python setup.py test && sphinx-build -qnNW -b doctest docs docs/_build/doctest
```

This sometimes led to problems on Travis CI with the second sphinx-build run due
to "disappearing" dependencies after the example application was tested.

A solution that worked for invenio-marc21 (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-marc21/pull/49#issuecomment-281287103)
and that was integrated in cookiecutter (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/cookiecutter-invenio-module/pull/98) was to
run doctest execution in pytest, removing the second `sphinx-build` invocation.

This both solved Travis CI build failures and simplified test suite execution.

Note that this change may necessitate to amend the code tests etc so that things
would be executed with the Flask application context (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-marc21/pull/49/commits/09e98fc18fd5591dc65d806286e8da51b7e384b4).

issue_title: MessageID broken for Python 3; produces byte domains which can't find utilities
issue_body:
As discovered in [zope.app.form](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.app.form/pull/1/files#diff-cbfcb4b47015db9258707315ce537abaR66), when reading a MessageID field out of a ZCML file, the `domain` will be a `byte` string under Python 3 (e.g., `b'zope'`). But `zope.i18n` registers ITranslationDomain utilities with normal `str` names. Since no byte string can ever equal a `str` on Python 3, the utilities will never be found and translation will not happen.

It seems to me that either MessageID should ensure that the domain is a `str` (decoding from...utf-8?) or the context itself could ensure that its `i18n_domain` is a `str`. I think I like the latter better.

Hi @rmjarvis - I was wondering about making some failures from the config functionality more helpful.  In particular, I had a case just now here I told it to make PSFs from an input file with a name blah.fits.gz which (oops) didn't exist.  The error message was

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Library/Frameworks/EPD64.framework/Versions/7.2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/galsim/config/process.py", line 840, in MultiProcess
    result = job_func(**kwargs)
  File "/Library/Frameworks/EPD64.framework/Versions/7.2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/galsim/config/output.py", line 222, in BuildFile
    data = builder.buildImages(output, config, file_num, image_num, obj_num, ignore, logger)
  File "/Library/Frameworks/EPD64.framework/Versions/7.2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/galsim/config/output.py", line 427, in buildImages
    image = galsim.config.BuildImage(base, image_num, obj_num, logger=logger)
  File "/Library/Frameworks/EPD64.framework/Versions/7.2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/galsim/config/image.py", line 232, in BuildImage
    image, current_var = builder.buildImage(cfg_image, config, image_num, obj_num, logger)
  File "/Library/Frameworks/EPD64.framework/Versions/7.2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/galsim/config/image_tiled.py", line 157, in buildImage
    xsize=self.stamp_xsize, ysize=self.stamp_ysize, do_noise=self.do_noise_in_stamps)
  File "/Library/Frameworks/EPD64.framework/Versions/7.2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/galsim/config/stamp.py", line 104, in BuildStamps
    except_func = except_func)
  File "/Library/Frameworks/EPD64.framework/Versions/7.2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/galsim/config/process.py", line 840, in MultiProcess
    result = job_func(**kwargs)
  File "/Library/Frameworks/EPD64.framework/Versions/7.2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/galsim/config/stamp.py", line 317, in BuildStamp
    logger=logger)[0]
  File "/Library/Frameworks/EPD64.framework/Versions/7.2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/galsim/config/gsobject.py", line 186, in BuildGSObject
    gsobject, safe = _BuildSimple(param, base, ignore, gsparams, logger)
  File "/Library/Frameworks/EPD64.framework/Versions/7.2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/galsim/config/gsobject.py", line 268, in _BuildSimple
    return init_func(**kwargs), safe
  File "/Library/Frameworks/EPD64.framework/Versions/7.2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/galsim/interpolatedimage.py", line 270, in __init__
    image = galsim.fits.read(image, hdu=hdu)
  File "/Library/Frameworks/EPD64.framework/Versions/7.2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/galsim/fits.py", line 792, in read
    hdu_list, fin = _read_file(file_name, dir, file_compress)
  File "/Library/Frameworks/EPD64.framework/Versions/7.2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/galsim/fits.py", line 203, in __call__
    raise RuntimeError("None of the options for gunzipping were successful.")
RuntimeError: None of the options for gunzipping were successful.
```

It seems to me that it would be helpful if it could (a) recognize that the file doesn't exist at all and/or (b) say what file it couldn't unzip.  (If you have a config file that reads in multiple input files, you won't really know what's going on from this.)  What do you think?

issue_title: Allow manual failovers when master is not present
issue_body:
At the moment, when master is not present and the replicas cannot promote themselves for some reason (i.e. being to far from the last known master position) there is no way to force the promotion of one of the replicas. We can introduce the `failover` command that does exactly this (checking that the master is not present), and rename our existing command that does the controlled switchover when the master is present to `switchover`.

This would also address https://github.com/zalando/patroni/issues/520

issue_title: Problem passing in lists for repeated fields
issue_body:
I am encountering an error whenever I pass a list of Protocol Buffer objects in as the value for a repeated field. The error originates in the TypedList ``extend`` method. I am expecting to be able to pass in a list of the appropriate objects. Do I need to wrap the objects in some kind of Pyrobuf TypedList object before passing them in to the constructors?

I have crafted up a short example that demonstrates the issue I am getting.

The protocol buffers content used with this example is contained in the code block below. This is a cut-down version of the original file that contains only the items necessary to demonstrate the problem.
It should be placed into a file called ``prometheus_metrics.proto`` and installed using the standard pyrobuf installed method.

```
message LabelPair {
  optional string name  = 1;
  optional string value = 2;
}

message Counter {
  optional double value = 1;
}

message Metric {
  repeated LabelPair label        = 1;
  optional Counter   counter      = 2;
}
```

``` python
import argparse

from prometheus_metrics_proto import Counter, LabelPair, Metric


ARGS = argparse.ArgumentParser(description='Pyrobuf extend issue demo')
ARGS.add_argument(
    '--work-around', dest='workaround', action="store_true",
    help="Use the work-around method instead")


if __name__ == '__main__':
    args = ARGS.parse_args()

    counter = Counter(value=1)
    label = LabelPair(name='host', value='localhost')

    if args.workaround:
        # Bypassing the constructor, I can pass in the list of
        # LabelPair objects and it now works as I expect.
        metric = Metric(counter=counter)
        metric.label = [label]
    else:
        # I am expecting to be able to pass in a list of LabelPair
        # objects to the ``repeated`` label field, but this causes
        # an error to be raised.
        metric = Metric(label=[label], counter=counter)

    print('labels: {}'.format([(l.name, l.value) for l in metric.label]))
    print(metric.counter)
```

Once the ``prometheus_metrics_proto`` module is installed then the example script can be run. When I run the script I observe an error, as shown below. This kind of error seems to happen whenever I pass a list of Protocol Buffer objects in for any repeated field item.

```
$ python extend-issue.py
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "pyrobuf/src/pyrobuf_list.pyx", line 64, in pyrobuf_list.TypedList.extend (pyrobuf/src/pyrobuf_list.c:3027)
AssertionError

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "extend-issue.py", line 26, in <module>
    metric = Metric(label=[label], counter=counter)
  File "pyrobuf/_proto/prometheus_metrics_proto.pyx", line 3186, in prometheus_metrics_proto.Metric.__init__ (pyrobuf/_proto/prometheus_metrics_proto.c:45950)
  File "pyrobuf/src/pyrobuf_list.pyx", line 67, in pyrobuf_list.TypedList.extend (pyrobuf/src/pyrobuf_list.c:3097)
Exception: type mismatch
```

I have found a work around method for now. This simply applies any list additions after the constructor. To run the example script using the work around method pass the ``--work-around`` flag.

```
$ python extend-issue.py --work-around
labels: [('host', 'localhost')]
value: 1.0
```

issue_title: Question about VVAR's TSB vs Vertical Origin
issue_body:
This is avery deep problem about variable fonts’ vertical metrics. The current mechanism for VVAR may cause problem if the point *being* topmost changed after applying a variation delta.

Let's consider the VVAR. We have a glyph with only two points, *z1* and *z2*,with their (default) coordinates being *y1* and *y2* (*y1* < *y2*),and the delta under the chosen instance being *δy1* and *δy2*.In vmtx and VVAR the TSB is defined as *t* + *δt*,and the advance height is *h* + *δh*. For CFF2 fonts, we also have VORG table that specifies vertical origin (*v*).

In the variable world,the advance height would become *h* + *δh*, which issimple. However the top side bearing become problematic now:

- The true y-max after applying a delta, **max(*y1* + *δy1*, *y2* + *δy2*)** may become not     representable in OpenType’s “value + delta” mechanism, especially for a font with multiple axes.
- For most type designers they want to specify the     vertical origin, rather than the top side bearing. However if they want to     do that, the top side bearing, ***v* + *δv* – max(*y1* + *δy1*, *y2* + *δy2*)**, becomes     unrepresentable.

So when generating the VVAR table for a variable CFF2 font, TSB and VORG, which one should be prioritized? What should we do if one of them become unrepresentable?

issue_title: datastore: No way to exclude_from_indexes new properties on updated entities
issue_body:
When loading an `Entity` from the datastore and updating it, there is no way to exclude new attributes from being indexed, since the only public API for setting `exclude_from_indexes` is on the `Entity` constructor. To work around this, I am currently setting `entity._exclude_from_indexes = (...)` in my code, which is not a "public" API.

Example:

```python
client = google.cloud.datastore.Client()
key = client.key('SomeEntityKey')
entity = google.cloud.datastore.Entity(key, exclude_from_indexes=('foo', 'bar'))
entity['foo'] = 'foo'
print 'entity.exclude_from_indexes:', entity.exclude_from_indexes
client.put(entity)

entity2 = client.get(entity.key)
print 'entity2.exclude_from_indexes:', entity2.exclude_from_indexes
entity2['bar'] = 'bar'
client.put(entity2)
```

Output:

```
entity.exclude_from_indexes: frozenset(['foo', 'bar'])
entity2.exclude_from_indexes: frozenset([u'foo'])
```

This is actually "expected" based on how the Datastore works, however, in the code sample above, there should be some way for me to put the entity back, without copying it into a brand new `Entity`.


Tested using:

* Mac OS X 10.11.6
* Python 2.7.10
* The following Google packages:
```
gapic-google-cloud-datastore-v1==0.15.3
google-auth==1.0.1
google-auth-httplib2==0.0.2
google-cloud-core==0.24.1
google-cloud-datastore==1.0.0
google-cloud-storage==1.1.1
google-gax==0.15.13
google-resumable-media==0.0.2
googleapis-common-protos==1.5.2
proto-google-cloud-datastore-v1==0.90.4
```

issue_title: Engine executions for multiple SingleAddresses are redundant
issue_body:
In many cases we're aware of literal addresses that may or may not exist (ie, they came in from the command line or via `--target-spec-file`). Currently those are represented as independent `SingleAddress` objects, each of which ends up being a root in the engine.

While the work will not be completely redundant, requesting transitive `HydratedTargets` for each of many independent roots will result in many independent dependencies sets, which then need to be merged for output.

To preserve semantics while improving performance, we should request transitive `HydratedTargets` for a container holding all of the roots, which will dedupe across the entire set.

issue_title: Attempts to test and install using python3 fail on ssl certification
issue_body:
When pip3 gets to installing the first package on the list, it chokes on SSL certification:

```
  Complete output from command python setup.py egg_info:
    Download error on https://pypi.python.org/simple/pbr/: [SSL: CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED] certificate verify failed (_ssl.c:749) -- Some packages may not be found!
    Couldn't find index page for 'pbr' (maybe misspelled?)
    Download error on https://pypi.python.org/simple/: [SSL: CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED] certificate verify failed (_ssl.c:749) -- Some packages may not be found!
```
The pip install command in pants lacks a --cert or --client-cert argument. I tested out adding this to a local cert file (fetched from curl.haxx.se/ca/cacert.pem) and got the same response.

I read up the documentation a bit further and found http://www.pantsbuild.org/setup_repo.html#configuring-the-python-requests-library which indicated to export `REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE` with a path to a local pem file. This also threw the same error. The same documentation suggest fetching a pem from certifi, which appears to be down.

I'd prefer not to fall back to http, or amend pants any further. I figured before I do, I should report the issues I found.

issue_title: GoDistribution should scrub GOPATH when not passing one
issue_body:
Currently there are 2 spots GoDistribution _adds_ GOROOT and optionally GOPATH to the environment of the `go` subprocesses it spawns:

1. https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/contrib/go/src/python/pants/contrib/go/subsystems/go_distribution.py#L95-L97
2. https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/contrib/go/src/python/pants/contrib/go/subsystems/go_distribution.py#L108-L110

Neither of these spots nullifies an existing GOPATH if the go command being run does not require a GOPATH.  This can lead to a GOPATH already in the environment leaking into pants `go` executions.  In and of itself, this is fine, a `go` command that does not need a GOPATH simply won't use it; however, if the GOPATH supplied in the user environment is invalid, newer Go will error out on what appears to be a global always-on check of valid GO env vars.  We saw this happen with a makefile that set:
```
export MONO_PATH:=$(shell pwd)/
export GOPATH:=$(GOPATH):$(MONO_PATH)
```

This resulted in a GOPATH of `:/vagrant/mono/golang/`.  The 1st entry is the empty entry and its not absolute as all entries must be, leading to:
```
...
11:27:16 18:27:16 00:00   [resolve]
11:27:16 18:27:16 00:00     [go]
11:27:16                    Invalidated 7 targets.INFO] Downloading https://github.com/Sirupsen/logrus/archive/master.tar.gz...
11:27:17 go: GOPATH entry is relative; must be absolute path: "".
11:27:17 Run 'go help gopath' for usage.
...
```

This was reported by @vinibaggio

issue_title: Disable/Prevent CloudWatch Alarms when creating a new DynamoDB table with SAM
issue_body:
I have several non-scaling DynamoDB tables created via CloudFormation. Each table auto-creates CloudWatch Alarms (and more for each GSI). In PROD this is okay, but in DEV this adds up in terms of cost. For example, for action table with a GSI I get the following alarms created:

```
action-ReadCapacityUnitsLimit-BasicAlarm
action-WriteCapacityUnitsLimit-BasicAlarm
action-siteId-lastCaptured-index-ReadCapacityUnitsLimit-BasicAlarm
action-siteId-lastCaptured-index-WriteCapacityUnitsLimit-BasicAlarm
```

My CF template is quite simple for each table. For example:

```
tableuser:
  Type: 'AWS::DynamoDB::Table'
  DependsOn: tablepage
  Properties:
    TableName: user
    AttributeDefinitions:
      - AttributeName: id
        AttributeType: S
    KeySchema:
      - AttributeName: id
        KeyType: HASH
    PointInTimeRecoverySpecification:
      PointInTimeRecoveryEnabled: true
    ProvisionedThroughput:
      ReadCapacityUnits:
        Ref: 5
      WriteCapacityUnits:
        Ref: 5
```

How can I disable CloudWatch Alarms for CloudFormation-created DynamoDB tables? Of course I would prefer to do this via CloudFormation templates itself, but since I am not specifying their creation, I am not sure if this is possible?

issue_title: Docs are missing discovery API explanation
issue_body:
I found the answer in the code, but would have preferred to find it in the docs ;)

In short, for those looking for an answer: Discovery service should return to url in _return_ parameter and return param _entityID_ for the chosen entityID.

On a sidenote: in order to shortcut the discovery service in test environment I pointed the discovery service URL back to the disco_response endpoint (/Saml2/disco in example) containing a preselected entityID. However, the create_discovery_service_request itself adds an entityID param as well, so there is some contamination that could have been prevented by choosing another consumer parameter for return entityID. I solved it by returning and checking for _eID_. Also, pysaml2 has a bug for discovery service url's containing a querypart, which I already created here https://github.com/rohe/pysaml2/issues/402

issue_title: `if imageio.get_reader(...): pass` fails for FfmpegFormat.Reader objects
issue_body:
The following statement:

`if imageio.get_reader('<video0>'): pass`

raises 'OverflowError: cannot convert float infinity to integer' (at least in Python 2.7)

It seems that `FfmpegFormat.Reader` has no `__nonzero__()` method (nor `__bool__()` for Python 3?), so the `__len__()` method is evaluated, which, in this case, returns `float('inf')`.

A quick hack for anyone running into this issue could be to use `if ... is not None`.
(see [stackoverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/a/7816439/4720018))

issue_title: Domain definition transformations should perform sanity checks on data
issue_body: `CategoricalDomain` and `ContinuousDomain` transformations should enforce that the values in the `X` data matrix have the same data type column-wise. Special attention needs to be paid to missing values (`None` vs. `float("NaN")`).

issue_title: Related content is not unidirectional, but rather bidirectional.
issue_body:
The issue is that we can't enforce the relationship to be symmetric in Django - so we are probably better off allowing these to be set, and proxying the 'related' property to be a unique combination of the related, and reverse_related properties for that object.

Current behaviour: We disallow these symmetric relationships.

Desired behaviour: Allow such symmetric relationships, and collapse the 'related' lookups and 'reverse_related' in a computed property that gives a unique queryset of all related content - regardless of whether it is in related or reverse_related.

issue_body: At the moment the title it output in whatever tag we decide to use on the frontend.  For the purposes of SEO it would be useful for site administrators to be able to choose what heading tag to use (`h1`, `h2`, `h3`, etc..). issue_title: Git repos on Launchpad are not handled properly
issue_body: In `charmtools/fetchers.py` the test for a bzr repo matches anything on Launchpad, and the test for Git(Hub) repos only matches GitHub.  This causes git repos on Launchpad to be fetched with bzr, which works, but seems to cause subtle differences, or may not always work.

issue_title: charm-build ought to run make lint after build

Note, it should still create the built charm and only be a warning if lint fails.

issue_title: Beta support for docker machine
issue_body:
It would be great to have a module `docker.machine` already integrated into this project once [docker-machine](https://github.com/docker/machine) hits 1.0 - machine is turning into a really fantastic project and their dev team is quite active. If you ask around on Freenode (#docker-machine) and look at their [Roadmap](https://github.com/docker/machine/blob/master/ROADMAP.md) you'll find that their plans include a client/server setup with a REST api that external libraries can use to interact with `docker-machine`, very similar to docker itself. That will be a while coming, as they are currently working towards [0.3.0](https://github.com/docker/machine/issues?q=is%3Aopen+is%3Aissue+milestone%3A0.3.0) milestone which will not address this client/server+REST setup. 

I have written some initial code to use `docker-machine` from Python. It's not optimal - it wraps the binary and uses subprocess. However, it works, and it allows me to use docker-machine from setups like Heroku by automatically downloading the docker-machine binary, creating a machine based off my environment variables, and connecting docker-py's client to the created machine. I'm well aware it's not optimal to wrap the binary (or to download random binaries from the internet....), but the code does work in a beta capacity. 

Question is - would docker-py be interested in a PR that contains my code to start test-driving beta support for docker-machine? My thought is that this could trigger other PRs to develop a more full-featured API for machine, with the internal codebase being switched to the server/client REST interface once it's available. 

[Here](https://github.com/hamiltont/WebJuice/blob/master/webjuice/utils.py#L52) is code to automate downloading docker-machine. 
[Here](https://github.com/hamiltont/WebJuice/blob/master/webjuice/utils.py#L102) is code to read environment variables and create a machine based off those variables. Pretty simple - any environment variable prefixed with `DM_` will be converted into an argument flag that's passed to `docker-machine create` e.g. `DM_DRIVER=digitalocean` translates into `docker-machine create --driver digitalocean`
[Here](https://github.com/hamiltont/WebJuice/blob/master/webjuice/utils.py#L173) is code that sets up `docker.Client` using the standard `kwargs_from_env`

Reading the spaghetti inside, I can see at least a few big issues:

* the dumb-return logic doesn't provide a way to figure out what the exact failure was.
* The parent-children iteration doesn't work as expected when symlinks are in the way. I mean, it checks only one of the parent trees, not the symlink target one.
* The code unnecessarily tries to force `+w` on all directories. Only immediate `mkdir()` parent needs `+w`, non-immediate parents need `+x` only.
* And at the same time, it assumes `u+wx` will be enough without checking the owner.
* `sticky_parent = (st.st_gid & stat.S_ISGID)` -- shouldn't this check `st.st_mode`?
* Permission resets are never executed when something inside the loop fails (immediate `return`).
* The post-reset `chown()` is terribly confusing. What directory is it supposed to `chown()` exactly? It looks like it'd catch the last directory which was `chmod()`-ed or `mkdir()`-ed. Please note that in case of race between two processes creating the directory, the final directory may already exist inside the loop.
* The whole thing looks like a terrible race condition candidate capable of messing up the filesystem good. But I guess that's inevitable.

I'd honestly drop all the smart ass logic there as it is just asking for trouble but that's just me. If parents are not `+wx` as appropriate, just let it fail. Trying to reproduce system logic for deducing `mkdir()` access is at least sloppy, and trying to work-around it is at least undesired.

issue_title: Added Haskell & stack support
store: Automatically remove nodes from Ceph on autoscale or IP change
issue_body:
Ceph expects nodes to have constant IP addresses. When nodes are added to the cluster, there's no problem - the Ceph services start and join the cluster. When nodes are removed (such as on an auto-descale), however, Ceph sees the host as down. This potentially becomes a problem once there are a lot of dead nodes that haven't been properly removed from Ceph, potentially resulting in Ceph not having enough healthy monitors to achieve a cluster quorum.

Additionally, when AWS relocates an instance to a new host machine, its IP is likely to change. When the store containers come up on the new IP address, they see themselves as new and Ceph creates new host entries. The old IP address becomes a stale entry in Ceph which will never come up again.

Removing entries from the cluster is done quite easily by following the [Adding/Removing Hosts](http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/managing_deis/add_remove_host/). However, this process is manual, and oftentimes I imagine users are unaware that their nodes are autoscaling or being relocated. We should try to find a way to detect these changes and automatically remove old nodes from the cluster.

issue_title: Builder: Make git sha hash available during build
issue_body: Heroku calls it `SOURCE_VERSION` (https://devcenter.heroku.com/changelog-items/630). This would be helpful for things like cache invalidation and some projects (e.g. https://github.com/drone/drone/blob/master/Makefile#L1) require access to the git hash during build phase.

issue_title: sync system clock before starting etcd/fleet
issue_body:
In our cloud-config, we sync the system clock using NTP: https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/dbd42a7352759f9ac98cbd59bf20ac757672bc6d/contrib/coreos/user-data.example#L46-L58

The cloudinit service parses and starts these services in-order, meaning that currently we're changing the system time after etcd has been started. I'm wondering if this could potentially result in etcd getting out of sync between nodes. We should move this service to start before etcd and fleet.

issue_title: Custom channel with HTTP auth broken in conda 4.2
issue_body:
Previously, it was possible to have a custom channel with basic HTTP auth using the `https://<user>:<pass>@<path>` syntax. With conda 4.2, this is not the case anymore. Instead, when such a channel is present in `condarc`, one gets 401 errors whenever trying to install or update something. I identified two reasons:
- Since `urllib3`'s `parse_url` is used instead of `urllib.parse.urlparse` (in common/url.py), the username and password are stripped from the `netloc` attribute and the information is therefore lost.
- Even if username and password are retained as part of the URL (e. g. by using `urllib.parse.urlparse` as I did for testing purposes), authentification is not successful, because (in connection.py) the `CondaSession` class sets `self.auth = BinstarAuth()`, apparently overriding the credentials given as part of the URL. If I remove the line, the HTTP auth works again.

Apart from that, #2804 is still true for conda 4.2.

issue_title: CDT metadata discussion
issue_body:
Prototype (not to be followed) for CI system is at:

https://github.com/msarahan/ci_test

Pay special attention to:
- build_platforms.d
- test_platforms.d
- versions.yml

These don't implement everything we need.  In particular, they don't really directly capture things like glibc version.  versions.yml is currently limited solely to the few packages that conda-build has hard-coded.  It really should support arbitrary packages.  This ties into https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1142

issue_title: Empty sets not handled by unrepr
issue_body:
If you write an empty set to a config file using unrepr mode, then it will fail when rereading it. This is because ast.literal_eval() won't parse "set()" and there isn't a literal for an empty set. 

I consider this to be a bug in ast.literal_eval(), but until that is fixed (and based on some bug reports discussion it may never be) it would be great to at least get a work around implemented here (probably in unrepr).

Thanks!

issue_title: Automatically trigger flush after append, pop, etc. for disk-based carrays
issue_body:
Hi, 

for discussion: each disk-based carray should auto-flush after reshaping events (append, pop, delete, etc.)
Why it should be done imho: all these events already impact the actual .blps and it's just the metadata running behind.

If you ever want locking with simultaneous writes you could even argue to rewrite the metadata before the actual chunks get changed (which you could assign in a lock), but i can imagine that being a headache with the leftovers. Anyway, that's a different (long term) discussion, but for short term auto-flushing really would be a great enhancement.

And yes, I was this stupid today :) (appended 2.2bln records from 1200 different files and forget to flush at the end -> 4 hours gone oops).

issue_title: Simplifying connect() function when not using .eapi.conf
issue_body:
I am using pyeapi in a context where I want arguments like username/password to come from the Ansible inventory (potentially from Ansible's vault) and not from .eapi.conf.

I look into the code a bit and was messaging with Ryan Madsen of Arista. connect_to function looks to be hard-coded to use the arguments from the .eapi.conf file. I then tried to just use the connect() method.

eapi_conn = pyeapi.connect(transport='https', host='1.1.1.1', username='admin', password='passw', port=8443)

After I did this, I would get the following message when I tried to execute my Ansible playbook. 

AttributeError: 'HttpsEapiConnection' object has no attribute 'enable'

In other words, I expected that I would have the same methods available to me as I had with the connect_to() call. I looked at what you did with connect_to() call and I could see that you were creating a Node() instance.

Ryan then pointed out I could do the following:
> cnxn = pyeapi.client.connect(transport="https", host="lf240", username="admin", password="foo")
> node = pyeapi.client.Node(cnxn)

I think it would be better if you did one of the following:

1. Have the connect() method return a Node object. I don't know if there are uses of connect() without creating a Node object (i.e. whether doing this breaks backwards compatibility). If backwards compatibility is an issue, then you could add a function parameter like 'return_node=False' and have it default to False so that the behavior only changes if someone calls it with the new argument).

2. Update the package __init__.py file and __all__ list to add the Node class. This would make it clearer that you potentially intended users to directly use this class (and then add a message to the docstring for the connect() function on how connect can be used with the Node class).

issue_title: add_source_file : Add argument to disable code coverage for selected file/files
issue_body: This is particularly very useful for bigger testbenches, where code coverage doesnt need to be collected.  By specifying which files need to be enabled/disabled for code coverage, the simulation performance can be drastically improved.

issue_title: Following redirects does not work in es-py
issue_body:
I tried my best to follow the documentation and code to figure out how to enable redirects for an ES client, but keep getting an HTTP 302 response for my request. Here is my code:

```
headers={'connection': 'keep-alive', 'redirects': True}
es = Elasticsearch([{'host': cluster, 'port': 80}], http_auth=(user, secret), scheme='http', headers=headers)
res = es.exists(index=myIndex, doc_type='test', id='ABCDEF1234')
```

Here is the error message I see:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):

elasticsearch.exceptions.TransportError: TransportError(302, u'')
