Based on the code in es-py GitHub under connection/http_requests.py, headers set this way below should have worked. I tried these variations below but without success.

issue_title: Can't access only authorized index
issue_body:
I have access to only specific indexes in an ES cluster. So when I do 
```
es = Elasticsearch("https://<server>:port", http_auth=('<user>','<passwd>'))
```
This raises an exception:- 
`elasticsearch.exceptions.AuthorizationException: TransportError(403, 'security_exception', 'action [cluster:monitor/main] is unauthorized for user [<user>]')`

So I tried to suffix the url with my specific-index 
```
es = Elasticsearch("https://<server>:port/<specific-index>", http_auth=('<user>','<passwd>'))
```
This works and returns me information about the index.
But then I'm not able to do any queries on it since the url is translated to 
`https://<server>:port/<specific-index>/<specific-index>/<doc-type>/<id>`

Is there a way for this to be like lazy evaluated? (I mean, the `es` object is created but not initialized until I run the query on my specific-index?)

issue_title: blobxfer getting stuck when copying a container with millions of files
issue_body:
I am trying to download the contents of a virtual directory on Azure storage to my local storage. The virtual directory has ~ 10M small sized blobs( < 15 KB each ). 

I am using the following commands to download the blobs

Command 1:
`blobxfer --storageaccountkey "STORAGE_KEY" MY_STORAGE_ACCOUNT MY_CONTAINER . --download --remoteresource .`

Command 2:
`blobxfer --storageaccountkey "STORAGE_KEY" MY_STORAGE_ACCOUNT MY_CONTAINER . --download --remoteresource . --include 'test/*'`

After I type in the command I get stuck at 

"attempting to copy entire container pictures to ." 

I've been stuck here for 30 mins. The same operation starts the download instantaneously while using AZcopy on ubuntu. Maybe the command is trying to compute the total number of files. If that's the case then Is there a way to skip that?

issue_title: Warn users about invalid values for `OneToManyField`s
issue_body:
When instantiating an `Architecture`, a user might write the following:

```python
org = Organization(id=1)
arch = Architecture(
    name='my arch',
    organization=org,
)
```

Or, they might write the following:

```python
arch = Architecture(id=5).read()
arch.organization = org
```

There's a bug in all of the above code, though. Here's the corrected first example:

```python
org = Organization(id=1)
arch = Architecture(
    name='my arch',
    organization=[org],
)
```

And here's the corrected second example:

```python
arch = Architecture(id=5).read()
arch.organization = [org]
```

The above bug will bite the user when they call `create` or its cousins. It will also bite the user when `EntityCreateMixin` is implemented and users call `update` or its cousins. NailGun should be more user-friendly by somehow warning the user about their mistake.

How should this be done? I'm not sure. One approach that _might_ work is to add a set of properties to a class when it is instantiated. For each `OneToManyField`, one property (having the same name as the field) could be added, and if a user assigns a non-iterable value to a `OneToManyField`, then a warning or exception could be raised. That would be nice because the user is alerted right when the questionable assignment is made.

Another approach is to extend `nailgun.entity_mixins.Entity.__init__`. The method already contains the following logic:

```python
# Check that a valid set of field values has been passed in.
if not set(kwargs.keys()).issubset(self._fields.keys()):
    raise NoSuchFieldError(
        'Valid fields are {0}, but received {1} instead.'.format(
            self._fields.keys(), kwargs.keys()
        )
    )
```

That logic could be extended to check and see if a user has provided anything other than an iterable. This approach is nice because it's extremely straightforward. One downside of this approach is that users are not warned when the make assignments to existing objects.

issue_title: Allow site override in /apps/order/utils.py:OrderCreator.create_order_model
issue_body:
Hi there!

I'm running into a site-related issue with the OrderCreator.  We are trying to pass in a site entity as an override, but the current implementation appears to ignore any provided value in favor of the Site's get_current() workflow.  Typically this wouldn't be an issue, however this is a multi-host scenario and we'd like to be able to indicate the site as part of the request to this class.  See here:

https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/master/src/oscar/apps/order/utils.py#L104

I'd like to propose that the setting of the "site" key be moved from its current location to after the "if extra_order_fields..." conditional on line 120.  We would perform a quick check to see if the site key had not been set via extra_order_fields -- if not we'd execute Site._default_manager.get_current() as is currnetly happening.

I am happy to make this change and submit a pull request to the django-oscar project if that's the best way to move forward?  If you could let me know that would be great.

Thanks!

Matt

issue_title: Ignore Mimetype of the challenge file

issue_title: Cell execution timeout when using nbconvert with wurlitzer
issue_body:
The default behavior to use the distributed wurlitzer [when available](https://github.com/Calysto/matlab_kernel/blob/v0.15.0/matlab_kernel/kernel.py#L24-L27) (i.e., on UNIX) causes intermittent cell execution timeouts when using nbconvert.

While I can copy paste the error log, deleting the module file --or better yet-- replacing it with the upstream one at https://github.com/minrk/wurlitzer seems to fix it. I believe the correct approach here (that also properly acknowledges the original author) would be to have wurlitzer as an external dependency. Unless of course there are custom changes in the distributed version?

issue_title: b2 upload_file to accept stdin
issue_body:
A basic unix way of programs interacting with programs is with stdin and stdout using pipe (|). I have a use case where I want to send a backup of a ZFS snapshot to Backblaze B2 cloud storage with compression and encryption. For example on a unix command line:

`zfs send storage@20160507 | gzip | openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -a -salt | b2 upload_file bucket-name - storage_20160507.gz.ssl`

This has many advantages:
* Local HDD space is saved by not having to save the ZFS snapshot to file
* Compression is leveraged using gzip
* The file is encrypted with openssl before it leaves the computer

I have a fork containing a working example of what I need with the B2 Command Line Tool: https://github.com/olcto/B2_Command_Line_Tool

Line: 412
m.is_true(tmp)
(C)ontinue, (A)bort, (S)top, (T)hrow exception, Invoke (G)DB
```

The second call to `check-sat` should result in `unsat`, as any alternative would violate the fact that `bvudiv` is a pure function.  As such, I'm assuming that an invalid model is getting generated internally, and then the fact that it's invalid is later discovered in the `get-model` usage.

While the first use of `check-sat` is basically a no-op in this case, without it Z3 correctly returns that the rest is `unsat`.

issue_title: Feature request: API to manually construct a Z3_model objects
issue_body:
I'm currently using components of Z3 to build my own solver (the parser and some of its tactics) but I use my own custom method external to Z3 to do the solving.

A problem I have is that when my custom method of solving finds a satisfying assignment I have no good way to create a `Z3_model` object. I need to have the found assignment in this form because I need to feed this model through the Z3 tactics I have used to perform model conversion so that I can give a satisfying assignment to the original problem given to my solver.

The only way I can see of creating a `Z3_model` in my case is to create a new solver and assert the satisfying assignment as a set of equalities and then ask the solver to find a satisfying assignment.
This approach seems pretty wasteful so what I really need is an API that would just let me build a `Z3_model`. In my case I'm working in the `QF_BV`, `QF_FPBV`, and `QF_FP` theories so the model I need to create is free from functions (that need an interpretation) and infinite datatypes.

Could something like this be implemented?

issue_title: Invalid model generated without warning
issue_body:
I've been trying to track down an issue I've been getting where Z3 seems to be generating invalid models. I became suspicious when my tool tried to use Z3 generated models in the tools expression language and finding that they didn't seem to satisfy the constraints I had passed to Z3.

I'm currently using Z3 via its C API.

I thought doing  `Z3_global_param_set("model_validate", "true")` would detect problems like this but no warnings or errors seem to fire. Seeing as I was very suspicious of this I decided to implement manual checking of a `Z3_model` in my tool (see https://github.com/delcypher/klee/blob/z3_debugging/lib/Solver/Z3Solver.cpp#L347 ).

Basically I iterate over the assertions in the solver and try evaluating the expressions using the model. Each assertion should evaluate to `Z3_L_TRUE`. However when running my tool I've come across a scenario where one of the assertions in the model evaluates to false in the provided model.

When this occurs I have my tool dump the query and the model and this is what it shows.

issue_title: Nonlinearities as arbitrary Theano expressions
issue_body:
Hi, perhaps this is already widely known, but it recently occurred to me that nonlinearities are a somewhat hackish way to introduce arbitrary Theano expressions into your network graph.  In particular, the NonlinearityLayer can be used to compute any sort of theano expression you want between two layers; e.g. in this questionable use-case:

```Python
l_in = lasagne.layers.InputLayer((2, 3))
l_print = lasagne.layers.NonlinearityLayer(
    l_in, nonlinearity=lambda x: theano.printing.Print('x: ')(x))
out = lasagne.layers.get_output(l_print)
compute_out = theano.function([l_in.input_var], out)
compute_out(np.array([[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]], dtype=theano.config.floatX))
```

will result in this being printed out:

```
x:  __str__ = [[ 1.  2.  3.]
 [ 4.  5.  6.]]
```

This is true for any layer with a nonlinearity; here we are printing and using a Tanh nonlinearity both:

```Python
l_in = lasagne.layers.InputLayer((2, 3))
l_out = lasagne.layers.DenseLayer(
    l_in, 4, nonlinearity=lambda x: theano.printing.Print('x: ')(T.tanh(x)))
out = lasagne.layers.get_output(l_out)
compute_out = theano.function([l_in.input_var], out)
compute_out(np.array([[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]], dtype=theano.config.floatX))
```

If this is something we'd like to encourage, I'd suggest putting it in an FAQ list #298 somewhere under "How can I include arbitrary calculations in Lasagne networks?" and maybe even making an alias for NonlinearityLayer called ExpressionLayer (or something) with self.nonlinearity renamed self.expression.  Note that you can also do this in a maybe less hacky but a bit more cumbersome way by calling `lasagne.layers.get_output`, modifying the resulting Theano expression, and using it as the input variable to a new `InputLayer` that then feeds the rest of your network.

issue_title: [jext] Directory Listing Styling
issue_body:
Some feedback I've gotten from folks about the directory listing, that I agree with:

* Make the font of the directory listing smaller than it currently is (pretty easy)
* People like the directory listing UI that is in commuter
  * I originally intended to use the same component, then realized I couldn't do a straight `mv` because of differences in routing (`Link` from next.js).

My general recommendation is to create a directory listing package as part of the monorepo, creating them as compound components like this:

```jsx
<DirectoryListing>
  <Entry>
    <Entry.Icon type="notebook" />
    <Entry.Name>
      <a href="..."> 
    </Entry.Name>
    <Entry.Time>
      <TimeAgo date={row.last_modified} />
    </Entry.Time>
  </Entry>
</DirectoryListing>
```

issue_title: Raw HTML in MD cells
issue_body:
Working on this but got stuck, so posting an issue.

Right now typing raw html into a MD cell will not do what you expect: render that HTML as HTML. Instead you see what you typed.

We use `remark-react` and `remark` for the MD cells. The [remark-react demo](http://mapbox.github.io/remark-react/) does the same thing as `nteract` in terms of raw HTML in MD.

I think what happens is that the `entities` option of `remark-react` escapes `&` to `&` etc. There doesn't seem to be an [option](https://github.com/mapbox/remark-react#configuration) to disable that. Not quite sure yet how all this is meant to work with their `sanitize` option which looks as it if it is expressly for people wanting to allow HTML in their MD.

issue_title: odo 0.4.0 should require toolz 0.7.3
issue_body:
Importing odo 0.4.0 just now gave an error traced back to:

```text
File ".../python2.7/site-packages/odo-0.4.0-py2.7.egg/odo/chunks.py", line 5, in <module>
    from toolz import memoize, first, peek
ImportError: cannot import name peek
```

It appears that `peek` was introduced to toolz in v0.7.3, but odo's requirements still specify >= 0.7.2 for toolz. Bumping the required version to >= 0.7.3 should fix this problem.

issue_title: Exception when NETCONF response contains an XML text node > 10,000,000 bytes
issue_body:
By default, libxml2 >= 2.7 restricts XML text nodes to a maximum of 10,000,000 bytes (~9.5Mbytes). In turn, lxml uses this libxml2 default, but offers the `huge_tree` option:
http://lxml.de/parsing.html#parser-options

If a NETCONF response contains an XML text node with > 10,000,000 bytes this causes the following exception to be raised:
```
    return self._conn.rpc(rpc_cmd_e)._NCElement__doc

    raise_mode=self._raise_mode).request(*args, **kwds)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/ncclient/operations/third_party/juniper/rpc.py", line 44, in request
    return self._request(rpc)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/ncclient/operations/rpc.py", line 339, in _request
    return NCElement(self._reply, self._device_handler.transform_reply())
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/ncclient/xml_.py", line 153, in __init__
    self.__doc = self.remove_namespaces(self.__result)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/ncclient/xml_.py", line 201, in remove_namespaces
    self.__root = etree.fromstring(str(self.__transform(etree.parse(StringIO(str(rpc_reply))))))

  File "src/lxml/parser.pxi", line 1828, in lxml.etree._parseDocument (src/lxml/lxml.etree.c:118072)

issue_title: Implementation appears not to comply with Section 5.2 of RFC6238 - security issue?
issue_body:
According to [Section 5.2 of RFC6238](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6238#page-7),

> Note that a prover may send the same OTP inside a given time-step window multiple times to a verifier.  The verifier MUST NOT accept the second attempt of the OTP after the successful validation has been issued for the first OTP, which ensures one-time only use of an OTP.

 However, the existing implementation (v2.2.6) appears to violate this:
```
$ python
Python 3.5.2 (default, Nov 23 2017, 16:37:01) 
[GCC 5.4.0 20160609] on linux
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import pyotp; key = pyotp.random_base32(length=20)
>>> t = pyotp.TOTP(key)
>>> t.now()
'925243'
>>> t.verify('925243')
True
>>> t.verify('925243')
True
>>> t.verify('925243')
True
>>> t.verify('925243')
True
>>> 
```
This could be a security issue: an attacker could conceivably MITM the connection between the verifier and provider, get the credentials & TOTP value, and use those to log in within the 30-second window. This would appear to defeat the verification because the TOTP can be replayed.

Alternatively, an attacker could shoulder-surf the victim and then log in separately within 30 seconds.

issue_title: Get rid of `make build-requirements`
issue_body:
During a conversation with @peterbe, we realized there were several ways of managing requirements.

Obviously, we agreed on the main necessity: reproducibility and environments consistency. We want to control the dependencies versions going to production.

Currently, in Kinto we have:
* the top-level dependencies (with optional range) in `setup.py`
* the whole dependencies list and their versions in `requirements.txt`
* we deploy kinto using the constraints of `requirements.txt` to keep deps under control
* pyup that opens a PR when a new dependency is available (which runs the tests)

So far, so good.

What was discussed is the fact that:

* The requirements.txt constraints are not used to run the tests on TravisCI
* The requirements.txt constraints are not used to initialize the developer local virtualenv
* We don't edit requirements.txt constraints manually. We run `make build-requirements` during the release process (which basically runs `pip install + freeze` to take the latest versions in range of `setup.py`) and rely on unit-tests (NB. we have 100% coverage)

This leads to situations where:

* Newcomers will fail at installing their dev environment because of an undesired upgrade
* Undesired upgrades can block unrelated contributions.
  * Example:
  * A contributor pushes some documentation changes, no change to the code base
  * CI fails because a backward incompatible upgrade was released and makes the code fail
  * We open a PR to fix the compatible version range in `setup.py`, and merge.
  * We can then merge/rebase the documentation PR
* Some dependency upgrade was not never ran locally by any developer. The dependency upgrade was just tested in unit/integration tests, and deployed.

Alternatively we could:
* Use the requirements constraints in dev + CI
* Remove the  `make build-requirements` part and let individual developers (or pyup) upgrade dependencies by editing `requirements.txt` manually (or using something like `hashin`)

Thoughts?

issue_title: Ensure http headers added automatically only if explicitly requested.
issue_body:
Currently, on load the `http_headers` block is always set automatically to default http headers if parsing records. This is incorrect for non-http WARC records.

Instead, by default only add http headers for `response`, `request`, `revisit` if length > 0, otherwise set `http_headers=None`

Sometimes it is useful to auto-generated the http headers for other record types, for example, for replay. This can now be enabled with a new `ensure_http_headers=True` flag, which will auto-create http headers suitable for replay with status 200, and content type and content length set.

issue_title: Constraint information is not being cached
issue_body: In `RedshiftDialect`, there are three methods, `_get_all_relation_info`, `_get_all_column_info`, and `_get_all_constraint_info`, which query information about the whole cluster. These methods are called by several other methods which pass their `info_cache` argument down to one of the above methods. These methods, in turn, are called by other methods which, in most cases, pass down all of their keyword arguments. The one exception is `_get_redshift_constraints` - this method is never called with the keyword arguments of its caller, and therefore never gets the `info_cache` object that was passed in by SQLAlchemy. The result of this is that constraint information is never cached. When running something like alembic which does a lot of reflection, this can slow things down considerably because the dialect winds up querying information about the entire database every time alembic asks for anything.

issue_title: Numerous auth errors during class
issue_body:
Earlier today I had a class of about 25 students do a dry-run of submitting an assignment through okpy today, and almost all attempts failed with errors from okpy.  My attempts during class also mostly failed, though I have no problems running it by myself before or after.  I have a few ideas about what might have caused it, and I'm wondering if you can provide some help understanding this:

- I had students going through the steps together, waiting for people to catch up in many cases, and so there may have been a minute or so between most students receiving their auth token and pasting it into their notebooks (we're using Jupyter).  Is there a short time limit on the validity of those tokens?
- Or perhaps the fact that up to 25 auth requests were coming all at once from the same IP (a jupyterhub server behind a campus NAT) could cause a problem?
- Or finally, is it possibly just a load issue caused by 25 students trying to authorize at once?  This seems least likely.

Thanks for any help or ideas you can provide.

issue_title: Execute cell with its pre-defined kernel
issue_body:
Now that each cell has kernel as its meta information, we should be able to execute them with the pre-used kernel. The easiest thing to do is to seems to redefine cell execution code, to some thing like

1. Get kernel from cell meta data 
2. Execute the code by automatically adding

```
%with kernel
```
to the code.

If this is difficult, we could try to 

1. Get kernel information
2. Before executing any code, use the `Comm` channel to send kernel information to the kernel.
3. The kernel would switch kernel before executing the code.

The difficulty is how to trigger the `Comm` communication before executing the code.

I suppose we can ask the jupyter guys how to do this if we cannot figure out a good way.

issue_title: Depends on dynamically generated targets.
issue_body:
Not necessarily dynamic, just some target that cannot be determined by static analysis. For example

```
[10]
input: 'a.txt'
output: "${_input}.bak"
sh:
    cp ${_input} ${_output}

[20]
depends: "a.txt.bak"
sh:
    ls ${_depends}

```

fails because the output of step 10 is undetermined because of difficulties in identifying `_input`. Then step 20 cannot run because of its dependency on the output. Instead of raising an error, the correct behavior would be leaving the decision of missing targets after step 10 is completed.

issue_title: Implement prefix query operator
Special token index is verbose.
issue_body:
**Context**
Special tokens are frequently used for masking or padding or interpreting the model. It's important in a Encoder/Decoder context that the decoder and encoder share the same indexes for EOS, SOS, and PAD. 

**Problem**
Creating two fields, one for French and one for English, there are no class constants for the index of `eos_token`. The only way to find out the index of `eos_token` is per instance of the class (etc. `self.stoi[eos_token]`).

The code by default is not designed to guarantee that the French dictionary has the same EOS index as the English dictionary.

**Possible Solution A**
With setting the optional parameter 'eos_token' would it be possible to set 'eos_token_index'?

**Possible Solution B**
Vocab or Field constant for the index of special tokens.

issue_title: [Explanation] Why do we need this condition in the ResNet?
issue_body: In the ResNet model, I see this conditional statement "self.inplanes != planes * block.expansion", referenced in the following image. I am confused with this statement. From the original paper, it tells that in `Conv3_1, Conv4_1, Conv5_1`, we need to do downsampling in each layer. Since block.expansion is set to 1 and 4 in basicblock and bottleneck respectively, which indicates that "self.inplanes != planes" and "self.inplanes != 4 * planes" respectively, these two inequalities are always true according to the ResNet architecture and we only need to decide whether we need to do downsampling based on the value of stride. i.e. whether `stride = 2` or not. Can someone explain it to me? Am I missing something here?

issue_title: ``expires_in`` and OAuth2 RFC
issue_body:
Hello, 

[OAuth2 RFC](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-5.1) provides the following information on the successful response: 

- ``access_token`` (REQUIRED)
- ``token_type`` (REQUIRED)
- ``expires_in`` (RECOMMENDED; The lifetime in seconds of the access token. If omitted, the authorization server SHOULD provide the expiration time via other means or document the default value.)
- ``refresh_token``(OPTIONAL)
- ``scope`` (OPTIONAL, if identical to the scope requested by the client; otherwise, REQUIRED.)

Having this in mind some parameters could be common in the extra data for OAuth2:

- ``access_token`` 
- ``token_type`` 
- ``expires_in`` 

Having this in mind I I purpose that in ``BaseOAuth2`` we start having something like this: ``EXPIRES_IN_KEY = 'expires_in'`` and ``EXPIRES_IN_DEFAULT = None``. 

This can be used in [extra_data()](https://github.com/python-social-auth/social-core/blob/master/social_core/backends/oauth.py#L361) to save the lifetime in seconds of the access token complementing the  ``auth_time`` and making it possible to use [access_token expired()](https://github.com/python-social-auth/social-core/blob/9fb7a06a0618aa88d62b980392f77b5a5f91f98f/social_core/storage.py#L94) at all times (for OAuth2).

PS: Is there any reason why currently the ``expires_in`` is ``expire`` in most backends and also the name used for [expiration_timedelta()](https://github.com/python-social-auth/social-core/blob/9fb7a06a0618aa88d62b980392f77b5a5f91f98f/social_core/storage.py#L59)?
In my opinion, this method should be changed from ``expires`` to ``expires_in`` to comply with the RFC and backward compatible assured.

This discussion started in  [StackOverflow](http://stackoverflow.com/q/41466731/4470542) and I think it should be continued here.

issue_title: Suppress default providers from update status view
issue_body:
As part of the initial implementation of first-class providers, we have started showing the default provider as a row in the `pulumi up` status - see the last line below:

```
     Type                                      Name                       Plan       Info
 +   pulumi:pulumi:Stack                       luke-luke-dev              create
 +   ├─ cloud:http:API                         hello                      create
 +   │  ├─ cloud:function:Function             hello66382ec5              create
 +   │  │  └─ aws:serverless:Function          hello66382ec5              create
 +   │  │     ├─ aws:iam:Role                  hello66382ec5              create
 +   │  │     ├─ aws:iam:RolePolicyAttachment  hello66382ec5-32be53a2     create
 +   │  │     ├─ aws:iam:RolePolicyAttachment  hello66382ec5-fd1a00e5     create
 +   │  │     └─ aws:lambda:Function           hello66382ec5              create
 +   │  ├─ aws:iam:Role                        hello4c238266              create
 +   │  ├─ aws:s3:Bucket                       hello                      create
 +   │  ├─ aws:iam:RolePolicyAttachment        hello4c238266              create
 +   │  ├─ aws:s3:BucketObject                 hello4c238266/favicon.png  create
 +   │  ├─ aws:s3:BucketObject                 hello4c238266/index.html   create
 +   │  ├─ aws:apigateway:RestApi              hello                      create
 +   │  ├─ aws:apigateway:Deployment           hello                      create
 +   │  ├─ aws:lambda:Permission               hello-66382ec5             create
 +   │  └─ aws:apigateway:Stage                hello                      create
 +   └─ pulumi:providers:aws                   default                    create
```

Since the user did not write `new` associated with this, and doesn't think of it as a resource they are managing, we should (at least for now) suppress this line to avoid pushing complexity early in the experience.  

I'd suggest that we do continue to show any provider explicitly created via `new aws.Provider(...)` in this tree, as those correspond directly to something the user believes they are managing.

Please instead check against the subtitle mxf container uuid.

issue_title: Protect outputs from being clobbered
issue_body: If multiple tasks are running a command line program `foo` that always produces an output file named `bar.root`, each task needs to do something like `foo; mv bar.root bar_1.root` to prevent clobbering. This introduces a possibility that files will still get clobbered if multiple tasks are running unless the user manually creates a sandbox for isolation. This possibility that files get clobbered depends not on the user code but on where the task is running and badly breaks our 'separation of code and execution' promise. I think this is a strong argument for sandboxing by default. We need some way to protect outputs from being clobbered: it is not user-friendly to expect users to manage this. I'm advocating a 'soft' form of sandboxing, which still allows access to shared filesystems, but ensures that `PWD` is unique for each task. Of course this still allows determined users to clobber (if they write outside of `PWD`), but I think it would fix the issue for the majority of cases.

issue_title: "project-job trace" print nothing
issue_body:
## Description of the problem, including code/CLI snippet
Gitlab API provide trace endpoint for job (https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/api/jobs.html). I find I run `gitlab project-job trace --project-id 5623 --id 872795` and nothing return. But when I run `curl -X GET -sL --header "PRIVATE-TOKEN: xxxxxx" "https://xxx.xxxx.com/api/v4/projects/5623/jobs/860966/trace"`, I can find the output

## Expected Behavior
`gitlab project-job trace` should output the trace

## Actual Behavior
`gitlab project-job trace` should does not show the trace

## Specifications

  - python-gitlab version: 1.5.11
  - API version you are using (v3/v4): v4
  - Gitlab server version (or gitlab.com): {"version":"10.6.3","revision":"753d851"}

## Some investigation
I dig the code and find the root cause, `gitlab.v4.cli.run` method only handle four type and has no else statement. 

```python
def run(gl, what, action, args, verbose, output, fields):
    g_cli = GitlabCLI(gl, what, action, args)
    data = g_cli()

    printer = PRINTERS[output]()

    if isinstance(data, dict):
        printer.display(data, verbose=True, obj=data)
    elif isinstance(data, list):
        printer.display_list(data, fields, verbose=verbose)
    elif isinstance(data, gitlab.base.RESTObject):
        printer.display(get_dict(data, fields), verbose=verbose, obj=data)
    elif isinstance(data, six.string_types):
        print(data)
```

But `ProjectJob.trace()` return bytes (<class 'bytes'>), so display operation. And `requests.Response.content` is bytes and `requests.Response.text` is unicode. `project-job trace` is a little special, it does not a file downloadable or a json string as most of gitlab API does.

issue_title: 28 Icon Suggestions: Based on needs of web apps/sites

Here are some icon suggestions for you to review - along with a small description of why they would be needed in a web app :

* group: For multi-user operations/needs.
* man: For gender variety to the user icons.
* woman: For gender variety to the user icons.
* magic: For wizard like tutorial/welcome operations.
* notes: For notes. (There are file and book  but no notes.)
* link: Very important for any web project. (Yes, we've external-link; but this is a simple link icon to be used in toolbars, etc.)
* attach: Again, this is very needed for attachments.
* history: For version tracking, etc.
* charts: For stats, charts, etc. (The current chart icon doesn't look clear on smaller sizes, maybe something without a border?)
* table: This is the only missing HTML component.
* adress_book and/or vcard: To represent "contact" or "address book".
* show_lines or list: This is the only missing layout option. (We've icon-th, icon-th-large, icon-th-list... but only list is missing.)
* unlock: There is no unlock icon the lock icon.
* cloud: For syncing operations.
* unshare: For "undo" or "unshare" operations and to accompany the share/redo icon.
* scissors: For "cut" operations.
* copy: For "copy" operations.
* pin: For pinning or sticky commands. (Maybe a simpler and 2D looking one, as an alternative to yours?)
* rss: For RSS or to represent a blog.
* envelope_read: To represent an open/read mail.
* credit_card: For payment options.
* money or banknote: For payment options.
* coin(s): For payment options.
* wrench: As an alternative to cog, for settings related icon needs)
* clipboard:
* disk/save: For "saving" things.
* calculator
* world, earth, map

Thanks again for your already great work!
